candidates.direction_name: Restore The Best Measured PTX Grouping Window On The Accepted Surface
ranking_notes: Round 11's measured hot-band kernel proves the active dispatch is no longer the PTX branch that produced the best-known run, so PTX-local retunes are currently gated on a surface restore.
This family already has a PASS_WIN in the round-history ledger and is the cleanest way to make the live PTX queue coherent again.
It ranks above the grouped_rows=8 and auxiliary fallbacks because it restores the exact family surface that still owns the best custom runtime.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 12/100 has to correct a search-surface mismatch before spending more budget on PTX-local micro retimes. The latest measured run improved only slightly to 24.17396832 ms, but its NCU details show the dominant hot-band kernel is `bf16_gemm_v1_tensor_core_fixed_hot_band_128x128_kernel<(int)452>`, and the current launch path still dispatches that non-PTX sibling by default at `src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:2084-2091`. That means the recent PTX control and barrier-family rounds were no longer acting on the measured hot path that produced the 24.16435242 ms best-known run. The immediate next move is therefore to restore the historically best measured PTX surface coherently: put the PTX microkernel back on the active hot-band dispatch and recover the staging / refill ordering that belonged to the winning grouped-window branch, so later PTX-local families become auditable again instead of floating against the wrong kernel.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Search-surface drift between the currently measured non-PTX hot-band dispatch and the PTX microkernel surface that still owns the 24.164352 ms best-known runtime.
candidates.risk: Moderate. This is a restore, not a fresh exploit, but the current tree drifted beyond a pure one-line dispatch swap, so the recovery has to restore the PTX hot-band surface coherently enough to avoid mixing the PTX label with sibling-path behavior.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.164352 ms best-known PTX run
Kernel Name in ncu_details.csv for the dominant hot-band launch
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the restore does not put the PTX microkernel back on the dominant hot-band launch, or if the restored PTX surface still cannot recover into the 24.16-24.18 ms band after one coherent pass.

candidates.direction_name: Restore accepted grouped_rows=8 hot-band consumer ordering
ranking_notes: This remains one of the strongest still-open round-history restores and it is a genuine current-source delta rather than an absorbed no-op family.
It ranks below dir_01 because the loop should first restore coherence to the best-known PTX surface before dropping onto a slower alternate PTX branch.
Keeping it second preserves a live alternate PTX state if the grouped_rows=4 restore fails or flattens immediately.
candidates.hypothesis: If the broader PTX-surface restore does not recover the active search branch cleanly, the next best recovered state from `round_history` is the grouped_rows=8 consumer-ordering restore. The current queue still carries this family as an open representative, and it is materially different from another grouped_rows=4 micro retime: it resets grouped CTA mapping and consumer/export ordering together instead of nudging a saturated local schedule. That makes it the right second-ranked fallback once the loop acknowledges that the active measured branch drifted away from PTX.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Consumer-side ordering and grouped-row locality inside the PTX hot-band microkernel, especially around grouped CTA mapping and the consumer/export handoff.
candidates.risk: Moderate. This is a real branch reset and its historical band sits behind the best grouped_rows=4 PTX surface, so it is useful only as a measured alternate PTX state, not as the default if the primary restore succeeds.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the 24.164352 ms best-known PTX run
correctness pass rate across all 3 cases
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the grouped_rows=8 restore remains materially behind the restored grouped_rows=4 PTX surface without revealing a better stall mix worth following.

candidates.direction_name: Retune The Auxiliary 256x128 Hot-Band K-Loop Schedule
ranking_notes: This family comes directly from round-history rehydration and keeps one orthogonal geometry branch alive while the PTX queue is being repaired.
It stays third because the immediate problem is PTX surface drift, not lack of alternative geometry ideas.
If both restore paths flatten, this is the cleanest non-PTX schedule family to reopen without broadening into a full geometry rewrite.
candidates.hypothesis: The queue still needs one orthogonal non-PTX fallback from `round_history` so the live frontier does not collapse back into only PTX restore branches. The auxiliary 256x128 hot-band family is still one of the strongest recovered states with a bounded local knob: its K-loop schedule stays conservative while the branch already carries async staging and a larger reuse tile. If both PTX restores fail to convert, a small 256x128 schedule retune is the cleanest way to test whether the current plateau is really a PTX-surface issue or whether the alternate hot-band geometry deserves to be reopened with a tighter inner loop.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Compute scheduling and latency hiding on the auxiliary 256x128 hot-band path, not DRAM bandwidth and not another grouped-row PTX locality retime.
candidates.risk: Medium. This branch is orthogonal and reversible, but it is not the current default, so the upside depends on a historically slower geometry becoming more competitive after a bounded schedule cleanup.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the 256x128 schedule retune cannot get meaningfully closer to the active 24.16-24.18 ms band or if it reopens a broad geometry regression.

notes: Projected exactly three node_b directions into normalized search candidates.
